does: This paper proposes Privacy Protection via Optimal Parameters (POP), a machine unlearning method for language models that selectively forgets target token sequences without complete retraining. The core contribution is approximating optimal gradient updates to model parameters, inspired by the mathematical derivation of complete retraining but implemented efficiently. POP applies these gradient updates to induce the language model to forget specific training data while maintaining performance on other tasks. The method addresses the practical limitation that fully retraining large language models would be computationally prohibitive, offering a more efficient alternative that achieves both effective unlearning of target sequences and retention of general model capabilities.
connects: This work builds on the emerging research area of machine unlearning, which addresses privacy vulnerabilities in trained models through selective forgetting rather than retraining from scratch. It responds to prior unlearning approaches for language models that efficiently remove target sequences but suffer from severe performance degradation in subsequent iterations. The paper connects to extraction attack literature that demonstrates privacy risks in language models, motivating the need for privacy-preserving methods. It also draws on gradient-based optimization theory and theoretical frameworks for understanding how parameter updates relate to training objectives, extending these ideas to the unlearning setting where the goal is to reverse the effect of certain training examples on model behavior.
enables: This work enables more practical privacy-preserving deployment of large language models by providing an efficient unlearning mechanism that avoids complete retraining costs. It sets up future research into iterative unlearning where models can handle multiple sequential unlearning requests without accumulating performance degradation. The approximation of optimal parameters could inspire further theoretical work on understanding the relationship between gradient updates and training data influence in large models. The method potentially enables compliance with privacy regulations like right-to-be-forgotten requirements in production systems. Future extensions might address unlearning of more complex concepts beyond token sequences, unlearning without access to original training data, or combining this approach with other privacy techniques like differential privacy for stronger privacy guarantees.
